title: AI-assisted coding is exposing a divide among developers
description: Simon Willison shares Les Orchard's insight about how AI-assisted coding reveals a fundamental split among developers that was always present but less visible. Before AI, both 'craft-lovers' and 'make-it-go' developers did the same work daily. Now there's a fork: let machines write code and focus on directing what gets built, or insist on hand-crafting. The motivation behind the work becomes visible through different choices at this fork.
author: Simon Willison (citing Les Orchard)

title: Coding After Coders: The End of Computer Programming as We Know It
description: New York Times Magazine piece by Clive Thompson featuring Simon Willison among 70+ software developers from Google, Amazon, Microsoft, Apple. Simon's quote: 'Given A.I.'s penchant to hallucinate, it might seem reckless to let agents push code out into the real world. But software developers point out that coding has a unique quality: They can tether their A.I.s to reality, because they can demand the agents test the code to see if it runs correctly.' Article discusses Jevons paradox potentially increasing demand.
author: Clive Thompson (New York Times Magazine)

title: MALUS—Clean Room as a Service
description: Simon Willison shares brutal satire on vibe-porting license washing: 'Finally, liberation from open source license obligations. Our proprietary AI robots independently recreate any open source project from scratch. The result? Legally distinct code with corporate-friendly licensing. No attribution. No copyleft. No problems.' Willison notes it took a moment to confirm this was a joke because it's too on-the-nose.
author: Simon Willison

title: Meet The $9 Billion AI Company Reimagining Vibe Coding
description: Forbes profile of Replit CEO Amjad Masad and the launch of Agent 4. Replit raised $400M at $9B valuation (up from $3B six months ago). Agent 4 introduces a digital canvas for vibe coding where users can tweak mockups, app designs, and collaborate in real-time. Focuses on non-technical workers like sales, marketing, and small business owners. Customers include Zillow (600 seats, 7,000+ apps built), Databricks, PayPal, Adobe. Competes with Claude Code ($2.5B annualized revenue) and Cursor ($2B+ annualized revenue). Masad: 'We can't cure cancer. Replit is about making everyone a software engineer.'
author: Richard Nieva (Forbes)

title: AI should help us produce better code
description: Simon Willison addresses concerns that outsourcing code to AI tools will result in quality drops. Argues that shipping worse code with agents is a choice - we can choose to ship better code instead. Discusses how to identify which aspects of process hurt quality and fix them when adopting coding agents.
author: Simon Willison

title: Perhaps not Boring Technology after all
description: Simon Willison explores concerns that LLMs for programming would push technology choices towards tools best represented in training data. However, with latest models in good coding agent harnesses, he finds excellent results even with brand new or private tools. Agents can consume documentation and learn patterns from existing codebases, making them effective regardless of technology choices. Also discusses Skills mechanism and projects releasing official skills for agents (Remotion, Supabase, Vercel, Prisma).
author: Simon Willison

title: Organizational Context for AI Coding Agents
description: Software Engineering Daily podcast with Dennis Pilarinos (CEO of Unblocked). AI agents have taken on a growing share of software development, shifting the hardest problems away from code generation towards context. The challenge is contextualizing why systems work, architectural decisions, and sources of truth outside the codebase. Unblocked's context engine aggregates organizational knowledge from source code, pull requests, documentation, chat systems, and production telemetry. Discussion includes context engineering, reconciling conflicting sources of truth, permission systems for AI, and shifting bottlenecks in the software development lifecycle.
author: Dennis Pilarinos with Kevin Ball
